name: Day Night Cycle
description: A survival world-clock driver changes pressure through visibility, enemy activity, temperature, sleep rules, safe zones, and scheduled resource consequences.
design_notes: In survival games, day/night is not only lighting; it is a pressure schedule built on a world clock. Designers should define survival_phase_id values, night escalation, safe zones, pause and sleep-skip rules, deterministic event scheduling, and how temperature, hunger, thirst, AI spawning, crafting availability, and UI warnings react to phase changes.
design_purpose: Create a preparation loop where players gather by day and manage risk by night.
Make shelter, light, sleep, base defense, and route timing valuable.
Give survival pressure a predictable rhythm instead of constant uniform danger.
player_fantasy: The player feels time passing in the world and plans around the danger that night brings.

parameters.name: survival_phase_id
parameters.description: Current schedule phase used by survival, AI, lighting, audio, and objective systems.

parameters.name: night_pressure_table_id
parameters.description: Table that defines enemy activity, visibility, temperature, and hazard modifiers for night phases.

parameters.name: phase_warning_offset_ms
parameters.description: Advance warning window before a phase changes and survival pressure shifts.

parameters.name: safe_zone_id
parameters.description: Area that changes or suppresses night pressure while the player remains inside it.

parameters.name: spawn_escalation_rule_id
parameters.description: AI director rule applied when night or special phases begin.

parameters.name: sleep_skip_policy_id
parameters.description: Rule that decides whether players can skip or accelerate risky phases.

edge_cases: A player enters a dungeon, cave, or base where world lighting changes should not fully apply.
Multiplayer players disagree on sleeping, skipping, or accelerating the night phase.
Night starts while crafting queues, raids, temperature shifts, or AI spawn groups are already pending.
Pause, photo mode, or a cutscene freezes presentation while the authoritative world clock continues or stops by policy.
A save loads at dusk and phase-change warning events need to resume without firing twice.
A server restart or reconnect restores phase time but not spawned encounter, sleep vote, or safe-zone state.
common_bugs: Lighting phase changes but AI and temperature still use the previous survival_phase_id.
Sleep skip advances the world clock but does not advance hunger, thirst, crafting queues, or enemy despawn rules consistently.
Night warnings repeat after load because the previous warning timestamp was not persisted.
Safe-zone checks use visual base bounds rather than actual shelter or claim volumes.
Clients integrate their own day clock and drift from server spawn, temperature, or darkness rules.
Scheduled events fire twice when a load lands exactly on a phase boundary.
balancing_notes: Night should be threatening enough to change behavior, but not so punishing that players simply wait through it.
If sleeping skips danger, require preparation or tradeoffs such as bed safety, hunger, or multiplayer agreement.
Special nights can refresh survival tension, but predictable warning beats are important for fairness.
accessibility_notes: Communicate phase and incoming danger through text, icons, audio, and lighting, not darkness alone.
Offer visibility, contrast, or reduced-night-danger settings for players who struggle with dark scenes.

implementation_notes.unity: Use the same world_schedule_clock and survival_phase_id for lighting, AI, temperature, and UI warnings. Persist phase fraction, previous warning id, and safe-zone state, then route sleep skip through a service that advances dependent survival systems consistently.
implementation_notes.godot: A schedule autoload can emit phase change and pre-warning signals to lighting, AI, and survival services. Save phase progress and warning state so reloads at dawn or dusk do not duplicate spawn escalation or skip events.
implementation_notes.unreal: A replicated world state actor or subsystem should own the phase clock and sleep policy. The server applies phase changes, AI director rules, and safe-zone modifiers, while clients interpolate lighting and display UMG warnings from replicated phase data.
implementation_notes.web: Keep the cycle in simulation state and derive presentation from survival_phase_id. For multiplayer or persistent worlds, base phase on server timestamp and content version rather than each client integrating its own schedule.

relationships.reason: Survival day-night pressure needs a schedule clock, phase boundaries, and save restoration rules.
Night and weather phases can change exposure pressure and shelter value.
Night phases can feed encounter pools, danger windows, and route-planning decisions.

example_games.title: Minecraft
example_games.note: Uses night to change enemy threat and shelter value.

example_games.title: Don't Starve
example_games.note: Uses day phases to structure survival planning and night danger.

example_games.title: Subnautica
example_games.note: Uses day and night lighting changes during underwater exploration.
